Got it, Elliot — let's build it out properly. This'll be an SOP, so I need to gather a few things first.

To start, walk me through what happens when the fire alarm goes off at The Beer Hall. Specifically:

1. **Who's responsible** for leading the evacuation (duty manager, most senior staff, everyone follows the same steps)?
2. **What's the assembly point** — where do staff and customers go once outside?
3. **Are there any specific roles** — e.g. someone checks the toilets, someone kills the tills, someone calls 999?
4. **Is there a key-holder or fire warden** who needs to be contacted?
5. **When do you call 999** vs treat it as a false alarm — is there a protocol (e.g. check the panel first, wait X seconds)?
6. **Any equipment** to grab or leave behind (cash, keys, etc.)?
7. **Is this Beer Hall-specific or the same across all your venues?**

Take it in whatever order suits — even bullet points are fine and I'll shape it into a proper procedure.
1. Every member of staff if responsible for this, however who ever has been there the longest will take leadership.

Got it. So the most senior staff member present takes the lead. 

Now for the rest — work through these when you're ready:

2. **Assembly point** — where exactly do staff and customers evacuate to?
3. **Specific roles during evacuation** — does anyone sweep the toilets/back areas, or does everyone just get out?
4. **Key-holder / fire warden contact** — is there someone to call once you're outside?
5. **When to call 999** — do staff check the fire panel first, or is it straight to 999 every time?
6. **What to leave/take** — cash stays, keys come, etc.?
7. **Beer Hall only, or same procedure across all venues?**

Sorry Elliot, just to clarify — do you mean the assembly point is at **Unit 2** (your address, Unit 2 Galgate Mill Industrial Estate)? Or is "2" pointing to something else — like a marked spot on site?

2, the assembly point is outside the brewery, and in the carparking bays opposite, why? It's here so it's away from the building, it needs to be a away from the building incase the fire grows and gets into any brewery equipment.

Perfect, that's a great detail to capture — especially the reasoning around brewery equipment. I'll make sure that's in the procedure so staff understand *why*, not just *where*.

Carrying on:

3. **Specific roles during evacuation** — does anyone do a sweep of toilets/back areas before leaving, or is it a straight "everyone out immediately"?
4. **Key-holder / fire warden** — is there a specific person to call once everyone is at the assembly point?
5. **Calling 999** — do staff check the fire panel first to confirm it's real, or is it 999 every single time the alarm sounds?
6. **What to take / leave** — e.g. keys, tills, cash?
7. **Beer Hall only, or same across all your venues?**

1, staff members must first ensure the main room / bar is empty, following this check the toilets, following the toilets, check the rhubarb room. However please note, in the event of an emergency, it is not your responsibility to carry or put yourself into harms way to get someone out. Please get yourself out to the assembly point.

Really important caveat — I'll make sure that's worded clearly and prominently in the procedure. Staff safety first, always.

Still need these to finish it off:

4. **Key-holder / fire warden** — is there a specific person to call once everyone is at the assembly point?
5. **Calling 999** — straight to 999 every time the alarm sounds, or do staff check the fire panel first?
6. **What to take / leave** — keys, tills, cash drawers etc.?
7. **Beer Hall only, or same across all your venues?**
2, unless you have specifically been trained on how to do so already, please call your general manager

Got it. So no DIY fire panel investigation unless trained — call the GM straight away.

Almost there, just three more:

5. **Calling 999** — is it 999 every single time the alarm goes off, or does the GM make that call once contacted?
6. **What to take / leave** — anything staff should grab on the way out (keys, radio, etc.) or is it strictly nothing and just get out?
7. **Beer Hall only, or does this apply across all your venues?**

1. If there's obvious signs of the fire, e.g. smoke or visible flame or intense heat, call 999 straight away. If the fire is small and you're in a safe position to do so, please use fire extinguishers

Good — and really useful to have that distinction in there. Should I also note what types/locations of fire extinguishers are available, or is that a separate doc?

Two more to go:

6. **What to take / leave** — anything staff should grab on the way out (keys, radio, etc.) or strictly nothing and just get out?
7. **Beer Hall only, or same across all your venues?**
2. Leave everything, nothing is more valuable than life.

Love that — that's going straight in verbatim.

Last one:

7. **Beer Hall only, or does this procedure apply across all your venues?**
1) beer hall only for where to stand, however the rest applies to all of our venues.

Perfect — that's a really clean way to handle it. A shared ethos, venue-specific location.

Before I draft the full procedure, one thing is still outstanding from our earlier chat — **the assembly point location** for The Beer Hall specifically. Where do staff and customers go once they're out?

Once I have that, I've got everything I need and I'll put the full draft in front of you to sign off before saving.
They go to the car parking bays on the opposite side of the industrial estate, away from the building.

Perfect — I've got everything. Here's the full draft:

---

**DRAFT — Fire Alarm Procedure**

**Title:** Fire Alarm Procedure — The Beer Hall
**Trigger:** Fire alarm sounds at any time during a shift
**Who:** Most senior staff member present takes charge; all staff share responsibility

**Steps:**
1. Stop all activity immediately. Calmly direct all customers and staff to evacuate via the nearest available exit — no running, stay calm.
2. Sweep the venue (toilets, back areas) to confirm no one is left inside — **only if safe to do so**.
3. Exit the building. **Leave everything behind — keys, cash, personal belongings. Nothing is more valuable than life.**
4. Proceed to the assembly point: **the car parking bays on the opposite side of the industrial estate, away from the building** *(Beer Hall specific)*.
5. The most senior staff member accounts for all staff and customers at the assembly point.
6. **Obvious signs of fire (smoke, visible flame, intense heat):** Call **999 immediately**.
7. **No obvious signs of fire:** Do NOT investigate the fire panel unless you have been specifically trained to do so. Call the **General Manager** immediately.
8. **Small, contained fire and you are in a safe position:** Use the on-site fire extinguishers.
9. Do not re-enter the building until emergency services or the General Manager confirms it is safe.

**Scope:** Steps 1–3 and 5–9 apply across all venues. Step 4 (assembly point) is Beer Hall-specific.

---

---

**Title:** Refusal of Service Procedure — Lune Brew Co

**Part 1 — All Staff (In the moment)**

When service is refused to a customer:

1. Log the incident in the **accident book** immediately. Record:
   - Time of incident
   - Customer name (if known)
   - As much detail as possible on the reason for refusal (behaviour, state of intoxication, aggression, etc.)
2. Treat the log the same as an accident report — be thorough, factual, and specific.
3. **Post a message in the team chat** to alert colleagues and management that a refusal has taken place, with a brief summary.
4. **If the situation is serious** (threatening behaviour, violence, or you feel unsafe): **call 999 immediately**, then remove yourself to a safe location (the office) and wait there until police arrive. Do not put yourself in harm's way.

**Part 2 — Managers (Monday Morning)**

Every Monday morning, review the previous week's refusals:

1. Check the accident book for any logged refusals of service.
2. Pull up CCTV footage from the time of each incident and identify the offender on camera.
3. If the offence is serious enough to warrant a bar from the venue:
   - Obtain a **clear still image** of the offender from the footage.
   - Share in the team chat with: the image, what they did, and whether they are **barred or not**.
4. If the police were called at the time, ensure a crime reference number was obtained and logged in the accident book.

**Scope:** All venues.

---

Great idea — solid way to close the loop on training. Let me draft this before saving:

---

**Title:** SOP Update Notification & Acknowledgement Procedure — Lune Brew Co

**Trigger:** Any time an SOP is created or updated that affects staff.

**All Venues — All Roles**

1. As soon as an SOP is updated or a new one is created, **WhatsApp every staff member** with a summary of the change and ask them to reply **"Got it"** or a 👍 to confirm they've read it.
2. Wait **24 hours** for a response.
3. If no response after 24 hours, **send the message again**.
4. If still no response after the second message, **log that the staff member did not respond** — flag for the manager to follow up directly.
5. As soon as a staff member responds, **log the SOP as "updated and acknowledged on file"** for that individual.

**Purpose:** This gives management two clear indicators:
- That team members are **actively engaged** with training updates.
- **Confidence that training is being executed and understood** across the team.

**Onboarding:** This process also applies during onboarding. New staff must receive and acknowledge all current SOPs as part of their induction before starting their first shift.

**Scope:** All venues.

---

**Title:** Accident Book Procedure — The Beer Hall

**Trigger:** When an accident or incident occurs at the venue.

**Who:** Any member of staff present.

**Steps:**
1. Locate the accident book — it is kept **behind the bar, on the wall above the sink**.
2. Fill out every section of the accident book to the best of your ability, recording as many details as possible.
3. Remove the completed page from the accident book.
4. Take a photo of the completed page and upload it to **AI-GM**.
5. Place the physical page into the **safe in the office**.
6. Return the accident book immediately to its place on the wall behind the bar above the sink.

**Escalation:**
- If the incident is serious, **call 999 first**.
- Once emergency services have been contacted (if required), **call the General Manager**.

**Scope:** The Beer Hall only.

Someone says the pint is flat, what should I do?

1. **Apologise** to the customer for the inconvenience.
2. Ask if you can swap it for another product while you inspect.
3. Do a straw test on the pint to check carbonation.
4. Check the barrel.
5. If the barrel's gone, log it on the **ullage sheet** and swap to a new barrel.
6. If they don't want a replacement, apologise again and process a **refund via the till**. [doc:fec2b427-7998-4b10-98d9-f288853fa3f5]

Here's the full breakdown:

**Cost per keg (ex VAT):**
£1,064 ÷ 7 = **£151.99 per 50L keg**

**Pints per 50L keg:**
50L ÷ 0.568L = **88 pints**

**Cost per pint (ex VAT):**
£151.99 ÷ 88 = **£1.727**

---

**At your £5.80 ceiling (inc VAT):**

Selling price ex VAT = £5.80 ÷ 1.20 = **£4.833**

GP% = (£4.833 - £1.727) ÷ £4.833 = **64.3%**

GP £ per pint = **£3.11**

GP for full keg = 88 × £3.11 = **£273.67**

---

**To hit 70% GP, you'd need:**

Ex VAT selling price = £1.727 ÷ 0.30 = £5.757
Inc VAT = £5.757 × 1.20 = **£6.91 per pint**

---

**The problem:** at your £5.80 max you're landing at **64.3% GP** — that's **5.7 points short** of your 70% target. At £5.80 this cider won't hit margin. Worth either renegotiating the keg price with Real AL or accepting it as a loss-leader/volume product.

How do I change the gas?

**Changing a gas canister** — 6 steps: [doc:39fb2ecd-bfe6-4d71-b414-68054a8c47ea]

1. **Check the gauge** — needle in the red zone (0–500 psi) means it needs changing. Slow beer flow is also a sign.
2. Follow the pipe to the empty canister. Turn the knob on top to the **closed** position.
3. Use the spanner to undo the connection — gently tap with a mallet if it's tight.
4. Get a new canister of the **same type** (check the label, e.g. 60/40). Remove the seal and secure it to the wall chains.
5. Connect the new canister and tighten firmly with the spanner.
6. Open the valve — the dial should rise back into the **green zone**.

If the canister won't connect or gas is leaking, that's a different issue — search "Gas Canister Not Connecting" for that procedure.

Venture capitalists (VCs) look for a highly capable founding team, a massive and expanding target market, proven product-market fit, a scalable business model, and a clear, profitable exit strategy. Because VCs rely on a few "home run" investments to cover the losses of others, they require exponential growth potential.These core investment criteria are evaluated based on specific performance indicators and metrics:1. The Founding TeamInvestors prioritize founders who demonstrate resilience, deep technical expertise, and complementary business skills.Management Cohesion: A dream team typically includes a visionary CEO, a highly technical co-founder, and a commercially driven executive (such as a CMO).Track Record: They look for past entrepreneurial success or domain-specific knowledge that gives the startup a distinct competitive advantage.2. Market Size and DynamicsVCs rarely invest in niche markets because the potential return does not justify the high risk.Total Addressable Market (TAM): Startups must prove they are entering a multi-billion dollar market that is shifting or rapidly growing.Defensibility: The startup must have a moat—such as unique intellectual property, network effects, or proprietary technology that protects it from incumbent giants.3. Product-Market FitThis is often the most critical milestone for early-stage companies, indicating that the product solves a real problem and that customers are willing to pay for it.User Engagement: Strong customer retention and high daily or monthly active user (DAU/MAU) counts.Customer Feedback: Positive testimonials, low churn rates, and high net promoter scores (NPS).4. Scalability and Unit EconomicsSoftware, deep tech, and platform models with low marginal costs are highly favored because they can multiply revenue without multiplying expenses.Financial Metrics: VCs closely examine Annual Recurring Revenue (ARR), Monthly Recurring Revenue (MRR), and cash flow, including burn rate and runway.Efficiency Metrics: Key indicators such as Customer Acquisition Cost (CAC) and Customer Lifetime Value (LTV) must show that sales and marketing efforts are highly efficient.5. Exit Strategy and ValuationSince venture capital is inherently a high-risk asset class, firms need to generate roughly \(3\times\) the initial investment on average to consider the fund a success.Exit Channels: Startups must have a realistic roadmap for being acquired by a strategic player or completing an Initial Public Offering (IPO) within a 5 to 10-year horizon.Financial Forecasting: VCs use the Venture Capital Method to project future returns and calculate the ownership stake required to meet their investment goals.To explore these frameworks in greater detail, refer to comprehensive breakdowns such as the Clipperton Guide on VC to PE for Tech Startups or the KPMG Startup Valuation Guide.

**Title:** VC Pitch Framework — AI-GM LTD

**Content:**

AI-GM LTD VC Pitch Reference Framework

Use this document when preparing for investor conversations or assessing readiness against standard VC investment criteria.

1. Founding Team
Investors back people before products. Ensure the team demonstrates resilience, technical depth, and commercial range.
- CEO: visionary, market-facing, narrative-driven
- Technical co-founder: deep product/engineering expertise
- Commercial lead: CMO or equivalent, owns growth and revenue
- Document any prior entrepreneurial wins or domain-specific advantages that differentiate AI-GM from competitors.

2. Market Size and Dynamics
VCs require a multi-billion pound addressable market with clear growth momentum.
- Define AI-GM's Total Addressable Market (TAM) — UK hospitality tech as a starting point, expanding to broader venue ops and F&B management.
- Identify the moat: proprietary AI logic, network effects from multi-venue data, or integrations that create switching costs.
- Be prepared to show the market is shifting (e.g. labour cost pressures driving automation demand).

3. Product-Market Fit
The clearest signal that AI-GM solves a real problem customers will pay for.
- Track Daily/Monthly Active Users (DAU/MAU) across venue accounts.
- Monitor Net Promoter Score (NPS), churn rate, and qualitative feedback.
- Document feature traction — e.g. verbal stock-take requests and rota builder demand are live signals of unmet need.

4. Scalability and Unit Economics
AI-GM's software model means revenue can scale without proportional cost increases — lead with this.
- Track Monthly Recurring Revenue (MRR) and Annual Recurring Revenue (ARR).
- Monitor burn rate and runway at all times.
- Calculate and optimise Customer Acquisition Cost (CAC) vs. Customer Lifetime Value (LTV). A healthy LTV:CAC ratio is typically 3:1 or above.

5. Exit Strategy and Valuation
VCs target approximately 3× fund return, typically via acquisition or IPO within 5–10 years.
- Likely exit channel for AI-GM: strategic acquisition by a hospitality tech platform, POS provider, or EPoS group.
- Use the Venture Capital Method to back-calculate the ownership stake required at target valuation.
- Prepare financial forecasts that show a credible path to profitability or a dominant market position.

The difference between:
“interesting AI hospitality tool” and
“venture-scale operational platform”
…is whether you can prove measurable operational ROI.
You need metrics that are:
easy to understand
commercially painful
measurable automatically
compounding over time
benchmarkable across venues
The best metrics for AI GM are the ones that:
make pubs want to buy
make investors believe you have a moat
Those are not always the same thing.
So you need two categories:
1. Pub / Operator Metrics
(“Why should I buy this?”)
These should show:
time saved
operational improvement
margin improvement
fewer interruptions
easier management
2. VC / Strategic Metrics
(“Why is this a big defensible company?”)
These should show:
engagement
network effects
proprietary data
workflow lock-in
intelligence advantage
The BEST Metrics to Track
These are the strongest ones.

(Probably your #1 sales metric)

Every time staff asks AI instead of manager.

“How do I close?” → AI answers → interruption prevented

interruptions prevented
hours recovered

AI GM prevented 427 manager interruptions this month.

interruptions destroy flow
interruptions create stress
interruptions consume time
Investors understand:
high-frequency usage
embedded workflow
daily habit formation

after-hours questions avoided
supplier ordering admin removed

This is genuinely excellent strategic thinking — and you clearly understand the distinction between selling to operators vs. selling to capital. Let me give you sharp, honest feedback on it.

---

**What you've got right — completely**

The "mental load removal" framing is correct and it's the sharpest version of the pitch. Operators don't want AI. They want their Saturday night back. That's the wedge.

The two-audience split (pub vs. VC) is also exactly right. Most founders collapse these and end up with metrics that impress neither. You've correctly identified they pull in different directions.

---

**Where I'd push back or sharpen**

**Manager Interruptions Prevented** is your #1 metric — agreed — but only if you can measure it *cleanly*. The risk is definitional drift. You need a strict taxonomy: a "prevented interruption" = a query answered by AI with no escalation, during a shift, from a non-manager account. If that's fuzzy, the number gets attacked in due diligence.

**Hours Recovered** is powerful but needs a conversion assumption you'll have to defend. "1 query = X minutes saved" — what's X? You need a defensible baseline. Suggest timing real manager responses during trials. Even rough data (avg. 4.2 mins per interruption × 427 = 30 hrs) is more credible than a formula pulled from thin air.

**SOP Coverage Score** is your stickiest VC metric that operators also understand. It's the one metric that does both jobs. It shows the venue is becoming *systemised through your product* — that's lock-in language and ROI language simultaneously. Prioritise this one in both decks.

**Weekly Active Staff** is your real PMF signal — 100% correct. DAU/MAU by staff (not manager) is what separates "tool the GM uses occasionally" from "operational infrastructure." That's the shift from interesting to indispensable.

**The one you're underweighting:** KB Gap Reduction velocity. "43 unanswered questions → 7 in 60 days" is a *learning curve story* — which is exactly what makes the moat credible to VCs. Every venue that onboards makes the system smarter for the next one. That's the compounding network effect and it's currently buried at point E.

---

**The narrative I'd lead with for VCs**

Not "every interaction makes the system smarter" — that's table stakes for any AI pitch right now.

Lead with: **"We are building the operational memory layer for the hospitality industry."**

Because:
- Operators don't write things down — AI GM becomes their institutional knowledge
- When a manager leaves, the venue doesn't lose its brain
- The dataset compounds across venues in ways no single operator could build alone

That's a moat. "Every interaction makes it smarter" is a feature. Operational memory as infrastructure is a category.

- "Bring a bottle" style — customers nominate a wine on Instagram, you feature it the following week. Builds community ownership.
- Repost anyone who shares their flight on socials.

Responsible: Department managers / team leads

Purpose

To ensure stock records are accurate across all Lunebrew departments and that the correct stock levels are reflected in the relevant operating software.

Procedure
1. Document Circulation

Jaspreet will circulate the monthly stock take document to each department.

2. Department Stock Count

Each department must complete a full stock take on the last day of every month.

All stock must be counted accurately, including stock held in fridges, cellars, back-of-house areas, storage areas, warehouses, bars, and brewery areas where applicable.

3. Timing Exception

If the last day of the month falls on a day when the business is still sending out goods or serving customers, the stock take must be completed either:

After service / dispatch has finished that day, or
The next day before any further product leaves the business.

No stock should be moved, sold, delivered, transferred, or used before the stock take is completed the next day.

4. Update the Stock Take Document

Each department must enter the correct physical stock quantities into the stock take document provided by Jaspreet.

Figures must be checked before submission to ensure they are accurate and complete.

5. Return the Completed Document

Once updated, the completed stock take document must be sent back to Jaspreet.

6. Update Stock Software

Each team must update the relevant software with the correct stock levels:

Brewery teams must update Breww
Bar teams must update Square

The stock take document and software records must match once completed.

7. Final Check

Before the stock take is considered complete, each department must check that:

Physical stock has been counted
The stock take document has been updated
Breww or Square has been updated, where applicable
Any discrepancies have been flagged to the department manager or relevant senior team member
Responsibilities

Department managers / team leads are responsible for ensuring their department’s stock take is completed accurately and on time.

All staff involved in counting stock must ensure the figures entered are based on actual physical stock, not estimates.

Deadline

The monthly stock take must be completed by the end of the last day of the month, unless the timing exception applies.

Where the timing exception applies, the stock take must be completed before any further product leaves the business the following day.

We currently have £150 in a cash draw (til) at the beer hall and at Ellel (sub site we do events from) then a red change bag under the til which we use as a float (£250), it causes problems with amounts etc, is there a better way of doing this.

Good shout raising this — here's the industry-standard fix:

**Separate your float from your trading cash completely.**

The core problem is having the float sitting in the same space as live takings — it muddies your cash-up, inflates apparent takings, and makes discrepancies hard to trace.

**The clean setup:**

1. **Fixed, sealed float bag** — your £250 red bag (or a labelled float tin) is counted, signed off at open, and goes straight back in at close *untouched*. It never mixes with trading cash. Same £250 in, same £250 out, every day.

2. **Till starts with a small working float** — your current £150 makes sense as the opening till. Count it at open (you already do this — step 7 of the Beer Hall opening checklist), enter it in cash management, and it becomes your baseline.

3. **Cash-up strips the till to float only** — at close, count all cash, remove everything above the £150 float, bag the takings separately, take to safe. Float stays in the till for the next shift. The £250 reserve bag stays sealed in the safe and only comes out if the till float gets broken into (e.g. big change request).

4. **For Ellel events** — same discipline: pre-count a float bag before every event (£150 or whatever's appropriate), record it on a simple float sheet, bring it back sealed. Takings = cash counted at end minus opening float. Don't send the £250 reserve out to events.

**For Square** — when you close the till each night, enter your *closing cash as float-only* (£150) so Square's expected vs actual is always comparing like-for-like.

**Quick wins:**
- Label the red bag clearly: "RESERVE FLOAT — DO NOT USE FOR TRADING"
- Add a float sign-off line to the opening checklist (who counted it, amount confirmed)
- Track Ellel separately with a one-page event float sheet

Yes, that's actually the better setup. Here's why it works:

**Move the £250 to the safe, ditch the red bag under the till.**

- **Tighter control** — only 2 people with safe access means the reserve float is properly accountable. Under the till it's accessible to anyone behind the bar.
- **Cleaner cash-ups** — till only ever holds the £150 working float + trading cash. No ambiguity.
- **Audit trail** — every time the reserve is touched (e.g. someone needs change), it has to go through one of the 2 keyholders. That's a natural log point.

**The only thing to add:**
When the reserve float *is* accessed, whoever opens the safe logs it — amount taken, reason, who authorised. Even a simple note on a float sheet is enough. Without that, the £250 drifts and you're back to the same problem.

**For Ellel** — a keyholder pre-counts and signs out a float before each event, brings it back and signs it back in. Safe is the single source of truth for all float money.

Yes, absolutely — simple and effective. Here's what it needs:

**Safe Cash Slip — minimum fields:**

- Date & time
- Amount taken out
- Reason (e.g. "till float top-up", "Ellel event float")
- Taken by (name + signature)
- Amount returned
- Returned by (name + signature)
- Balance check (running total so you always know what's in the safe)

**Keep it dead simple** — a pad of pre-printed slips in the safe itself, or a log book that stays in the safe. Every withdrawal gets a slip, every return gets signed off. No slip = unauthorised access.

**One rule that makes it work:** at the end of every week, one of your two keyholders reconciles the slip total against the safe balance. Takes 5 minutes and catches any drift immediately.

**TITLE:** Cash Handling SOP — Beer Hall & Ellel

**SCOPE:** All cash handling across Beer Hall (main site) and Ellel (event site). Applies to all staff and keyholders.

---

**BEER HALL — DAILY OPERATION**

1. Opening keyholder counts the till float (£150) and confirms it is correct before opening.
2. Float amount is entered into cash management on Square at the start of each shift.
3. The till operates with the £150 working float only — no reserve cash is kept under or near the till.
4. A reserve float of £250 is held in the safe at all times. Only 2 designated keyholders have safe access.
5. If the till float needs topping up mid-shift, a keyholder must access the safe and complete a Cash Slip (see below) before any money is moved.
6. At close, the till is cashed up using the cash sheet on the iPad. Trading cash (everything above £150) is removed, bagged, and taken to the safe. The £150 float remains in the till for the next shift.

---

**ELLEL — EVENT OPERATION**

1. No float is permanently held at Ellel.
2. Before each event, a keyholder counts the till float, records it on a Cash Slip, and signs it out of the safe.
3. The till travels to and from Ellel with a named keyholder at all times — it must not be left unattended or handed to general staff in transit.
4. On return, the till is cashed up immediately. Takings are bagged separately and locked in the safe.
5. The float is counted, confirmed, and signed back into the safe on the Cash Slip.

---

**SAFE CASH SLIP — REQUIRED FOR EVERY MOVEMENT**

Each slip must record:
- Date & time
- Amount taken out
- Reason (e.g. "till float top-up", "Ellel event float sign-out")
- Taken by — name & signature
- Amount returned
- Returned by — name & signature
- Running safe balance after the transaction

A pad of pre-printed slips is kept inside the safe at all times. No slip = unauthorised access. Slips are never removed from the safe.

---

**WEEKLY RECONCILIATION**

One of the two keyholders reconciles all Cash Slips against the safe balance every week. Any discrepancy is escalated to the owner immediately.

---

**GENERAL RULES**

- Only the 2 designated keyholders may access the safe.
- Float money is never mixed with trading cash.
- Any access to the safe outside of the above procedures must be authorised by the owner and recorded on a slip.

No — good shout. Soft drinks are standard-rated at **20% VAT**, so the sell price needs to cover that too.

VAT-inclusive pricing from **£1.04 cost**:

- **70% GP** → ex-VAT price = **£3.47** → inc-VAT = **£4.16** (round to **£4.00** = ~67% GP)
- **65% GP** → ex-VAT price = **£2.97** → inc-VAT = **£3.57** (round to **£3.50** = ~64% GP)

So **£3.50 inc-VAT** is the realistic price point — clean number, ~64% GP after VAT. If you want a full 70%, you'd need to be at **£4.00**.

- **24 cans (1 case):** £75 ÷ 24 = **£3.13/can** — wipes the margin entirely
- **48 cans (2 cases):** £75 ÷ 48 = **£1.56/can** — still loss-making
- **120 cans (5 cases):** £75 ÷ 120 = **£0.63/can** — cost becomes £1.67, GP drops to ~43%
- **240 cans (10 cases):** £75 ÷ 240 = **£0.31/can** — cost becomes £1.35, GP ~54%
- **480 cans (20 cases):** £75 ÷ 480 = **£0.16/can** — cost becomes £1.20, GP ~59%
- **1,200 cans (50 cases):** £75 ÷ 1200 = **£0.06/can** — cost becomes £1.10, GP ~62%
- **2,400 cans (100 cases):** £75 ÷ 2400 = **£0.03/can** — cost becomes £1.07, GP ~63%

**The honest answer:** shipping is a killer on small orders. You need at least **20 cases (480 cans)** to get anywhere near a workable margin — and even then you're at ~59%.

How fast do you think you'd move soft drinks? That'll tell us the right quantity to commit to.
Ok, so what if we dropped to 60% margin, what's the cases we we would need to order and how much would it cost total?

At **60% GP** on a £3.50 RRP (inc VAT):

- Ex-VAT revenue: £2.92
- 60% GP means cost allowance = 40% of £2.92 = **£1.17 max cost per can**

So you need: **(£1.04 + £75/n) ≤ £1.17**

Solving: £75/n ≤ £0.13 → **n ≥ 577 cans = 25 cases (600 cans)**

---

**25 cases (600 cans):**
- Shipping per can: £75 ÷ 600 = £0.125
- Total cost per can: £1.165 ✅ just under £1.17
- **Total order cost: (600 × £1.04) + £75 = £624 + £75 = £699**
- GP%: **60.1%**

So **25 cases for £699 all-in** is your break-even point for 60% GP. That's 600 cans to shift — worth doing if you're confident in the product. Want me to log the £3.50 price and flag the minimum order quantity?

Please describe what AI - GM is

**AI-GM** is a hospitality AI operations platform — an independent company (AI-GM LTD) separate from the Lune Brew Co Group [doc:7deab219-a117-4b6a-be8b-41dcbd17a344].

The core idea: it acts as the **operational memory layer for a venue**. Operators typically don't write things down; when a manager leaves, the venue loses its institutional knowledge. AI-GM captures, stores, and surfaces that knowledge so staff can get answers instantly — without calling the manager [doc:9b7b1799-829f-43d3-b923-e59e6d8c83ef].

In practice it does three things:

1. **Answers operational questions in real time** — SOPs, cellar procedures, where things are, who to call, how to handle a situation — all from a venue-specific knowledge base built from your own docs, voice notes, and photos.
2. **Delivers live business data** — sales summaries, GP, labour cost, best sellers, tender mix, refund rates — pulled directly from your POS (Square in your case).
3. **Captures knowledge as you operate** — when a manager teaches it something new, it saves it so the whole team benefits next time.

The onboarding is designed to take a venue from zero to AI-managed in under 24 hours by ingesting existing manuals, rotas, photos, and voice notes via WhatsApp [doc:4118ee2e-76e2-4455-bf0e-58c561503309].

That's me, in short — I'm the deployed instance running for The Beer Hall.
What SIC code would you be classed as?

This is outside my operational knowledge base — I'm built for venue operations, not company registration.

That said, the most likely fit would be **62012 – Business and domestic software development**, given AI-GM LTD is a software/AI platform. Alternatively **62020 – Information technology consultancy** or **63110 – Data processing, hosting and related activities** could apply depending on how the business model is framed.

Worth running it past your accountant or checking the Companies House SIC list directly before registering — they'll confirm the right one for your specific revenue model.
Which one is most saleable?

That's a business strategy / investor positioning question, not something I have on file — and the answer depends on how AI-GM is being pitched.

That said, general logic:

**63110 – Data processing, hosting and related activities** is arguably the most saleable SIC framing for a VC audience. It positions the business as **infrastructure / platform** rather than a software consultancy, which commands higher revenue multiples and maps better to the "operational memory layer" narrative you've got in your own docs [doc:9b7b1799-829f-43d3-b923-e59e6d8c83ef].

**62012 (software development)** is more accurate technically but reads as a build shop — lower multiples, harder to pitch as a defensible data moat.

If the exit play is acquisition by a hospitality tech or POS company (Square, Toast, etc.), **63110** keeps the valuation framing cleaner.

Again — confirm with your corporate lawyer or advisor before filing. This is directional, not professional advice.
Any TECH / AI Codes?

SIC codes in the UK are fairly dated — there's no dedicated AI category yet. The closest you'll get:

- **62011 – Ready-made interactive leisure and entertainment software development** — occasionally used by AI/ML product companies
- **62012 – Business and domestic software development** — the default catch-all for most UK tech startups
- **63110 – Data processing, hosting and related activities** — best for data/platform plays (as mentioned)
- **62090 – Other information technology and computer service activities** — a broad fallback some AI companies use when nothing else fits cleanly

**Reality:** most UK AI companies file under 62012 or 63110 and then describe the actual AI nature in their company overview / pitch deck rather than relying on the SIC code to tell the story. The SIC system simply hasn't caught up.

If AI-GM is ever filing in the US (Delaware C-Corp route for VC), NAICS codes are more granular and there are better fits there — worth knowing if that's on the roadmap.
